moduleContent: This module is offered to all incoming year one students excluding BA in Education & Design or Fine Art students and BA Visual Culture students, and provides an opportunity to: ● Learn how to locate, navigate and utilise key information and learning resources within NCAD; ● Develop a range of study, and digital skills to assist in their academic development and emerging professional identity as artists/designers/educators. This module addresses the development of students’ identity within higher education and wider afield as well as the acquisition of generic study skills applicable to research and practice. Students will be encouraged to foster attitudinal approaches appropriate to their formation as learners and practitioners. This module will provide an introduction to art, design, and creative practice as a mode of enquiry starting from the concrete needs of the student as a learner. It will provide students with an understanding of key concepts, develop their capacity for independent enquiry and their management of learning and assessment. As part of the process of developing a learner identity, students will use active research to develop effective use of the college learning resources for research and practice. Students will avail of the Library in the development of academic writing skills and practice techniques. They will learn to use the online learning resources of the College, and will begin to develop relevant digital skills with appropriate methodologies, for both their use as students and for their practice in art and design disciplines. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year CE1-2: Learning & Professional Practice 1A Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 24 Jun 25 Page 1 of 5 This document was prepared for NCAD students. moduleContent: This module introduces students to a range of Art and Design practices through a series of projects. Inductions to the three workshop areas in the first year department are delivered introducing students to these facilities. They will learn creative and research skills and the use of materials and processes in context, as tools of practical investigation and critical inquiry. Students are encouraged to develop a body of work through experimentation, drawing, research, making, evaluating, presenting and reflecting and recording. The module will be delivered through a series of tasks addressing the following key themes: ● Observation, Analysis and Research - Students are introduced to visual research methodologies through a combination of studio activities, emphasising drawing, making and idea development. ● Material and Processes - Students are introduced to processes, skills and materials within a given context as tools of practical investigation and idea development. There will be a summative assessment against the module learning outcomes at the end of the module. Tasks will be developed and reviewed annually by interdisciplinary teams from across NCAD, under the supervision of the Programme Leaders and Module Co-ordinator. The aims of this module are to: 1. Develop students’ ability to research, investigate and analyse art and design practices. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year CE1-4: Art, Design & Education Studio 1A 1 Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 24 Jun 25 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. moduleContent: This module will encourage inventive and intelligent experimentation with processes and materials in relation to problem-solving and conceptual development. Students will be expected to develop a personal direction in relation to path finding, through experimentation, making, evaluating, presenting and reflecting. They will be required to show initiative in carrying out project briefs and set activities. The module will be delivered through a series of projects providing opportunity to explore students’ fields of interest and addressing the following key themes: ● Concept/Topic - Students will develop research, and the use of media, materials and processes in the investigation of a chosen concept/topic. ● Negotiating the Fields of Practice / Path-finding - Students enhance their practical skills with an emphasis on idea development and interpretation towards choosing Fine Art or Design as your School of further study. In addition, students complete their module work in preparation for progression to their chosen school. There will be a summative assessment against the module learning outcomes at the end of the module. Projects will be developed and reviewed annually by interdisciplinary teams from across NCAD, under the supervision of the Programme Leaders and Module Coordinator. The aims of this module are to: 1. moduleContent: Students will be introduced to fundamental knowledge and language skills appropriate to their chosen art or design discipline. The module will be delivered through a series of projects addressing the following key themes: • Specialist - Discipline specific practice and theory • Consolidation - Reflective Practice, Documentation and Dissemination. These will be summatively assessed against the module learning outcomes at the end of the module. Projects will be developed and reviewed annually by interdisciplinary teams from across NCAD, under the supervision of the Programme Leaders and Module Co-ordinator. Aim The aim of this module is to introduce students to fundamental disciplinary methods, techniques and approaches.
loText: There will be specific discipline projects for each pathway and students will learn in their specialist discipline grouping led by First Year and School staff. Each student grouping will learn content specific to their discipline area. This content will be defined in project briefs.

moduleContent: This module introduces students to the working methods and professional practices within Design, Applied Art or Fine Art that underpin studio practice. Students will be able to choose between Design School or Fine Art School offerings of this module depending on their entry route and their choice of specialisation at this point of the academic year. At the midway point of this module students may change between Fine Art PP, or Design PP to suit their final specialist placement in their studio CE1-7 module. The module aims to: ● Introduce fundamental professional contemporary art or design practices and methods. ● Enable students to begin to position their interests within the field of art or design. ● Introduce a range of creative methodologies and strategies that frame professional presentation, exhibition and documentation of work.
loText: Demonstrate a recognition of the creative context for Design, Applied Art or Fine Art.

moduleContent: This module focuses on the development of skills and knowledge applicable to the formation of a professional practice. The module concentrates on the acquisition of skills and competency in the use of materials in painting. Students are encouraged to examine developments in contemporary art practice and become acquainted with a range of galleries and museums. The aims of this module are to: • Generate ideas and expand on these for project work. • Explore a range of themes using library resources and notebooks. • Document the research process and compile notebooks as a resource. • Practise technical skills in drawing and painting. • Produce a number of drawing and paintings. • Participate in small group discussion and peer learning.

moduleContent: This module seeks to empower creative practitioners from a range of disciplines, with the sustainable and adaptable skills and attributes that they will require to shape the future of Ireland’s creative sector. The module encompasses a network of experiential online and hybrid courses that act as platforms for deeper consideration of thematically-based and skills-led knowledge, for intensive skilling or for shaping and sharing critical dialogues. Learners engage in a set of learning experiences that build into a body of specific, clear and relevant knowledge which can be transferred directly to their creative and/or professional practice. The learner has an opportunity to test their direction, thinking, appetite and practice in a supported and connected learning environment that encourages continued, creative exploration. The module forms part of the Studio+ suite of modules, which seek to give students at NCAD a sense of independence and professionalism as they work collaboratively with each other, and with external organisations.

moduleContent: The aim of this module is to provide the student with the opportunity to apply the subject specific design skills, knowledge and understanding, as well as personal transferable skills, acquired in the programme, to the real-world environment of work. The module seeks to: ● enable students to gain real life ‘practical’ experience within their chosen area of study; ● increase student employability; ● provide students with a professional network within their chosen field. Students undertake a work placement and agreed programme of part-time work within a vetted company or organisation equivalent to 300-400 hours of work-based learning. Students will follow an agreed learning plan in order to receive meaningful, study-related work experience, which will be of significant advantage to them when in post-graduation employment. Students will record their experience in both a logbook, placement report and a reflective journal, and their performance at work will be evaluated by their respective in-placement company via an evaluation form. Delivery is by means of ‘on-the-job’ activities; in addition, students may be required to attend specific staff development activities, as designated by the host companies/organisations. moduleContent: This module is intended to act as a creative platform for students to explore a range of positions that complement and critique the traditional role of design for industry. The module encourages students to begin to position themselves within the future design landscape. Projects will encourage students to challenge existing codes and modes of design and foster student’s abilities to be curious, questioning, challenging, provocative, subversive, ambitious, resourceful, innovative, iconoclastic and as ecologically and ethically responsible as possible. The module is underpinned by lectures, seminars and workshops, and seeks to provide students with the opportunity to apply the subject-specific design skills, knowledge and understanding, as well as personal transferable skills, acquired in the programme, and address projects within an multi-disciplinary team for a trimester. The module seeks to: 1. Enable students to experience the expanding remit of design within a multi-disciplinary design team.

moduleContent: Art Practice Situations 1 is offered through an interdisciplinary context within or beyond NCAD. In this module you will establish your knowledge of a context or learning situation that offers you experiences and art capacities that are new to you, and that exist in different environments that are often developed through partnered engagement and access. Through your experiential participation, engagement, research and discovery you will gain understanding of your chosen situation/ environment/ thematic/ material and learn to consider the potentials of known and emerging perspectives through a variety of encounters and tasks. You will be supported by crits, presentations on and off - site research and digital or material engagement and tutorials. To prosper in this module, you are expected to establish your self-organisation, self-motivation to support a process that is experimental and integrating relevant sources, encounters and different perspectives. moduleContent: Art Practice Situations (2) is offered through an interdisciplinary context within or beyond NCAD. In this module you will evolve your knowledge of a context or learning situation that offers you experience and art capacities that are new to you, and that exist in different environments that are often developed through partnered engagement and access. Through your experiential participation, engagement, research and discovery you will deepen your understandings of your chosen situation/ environment/ thematic/ material. You will learn to consider and engage the potentials of known and emerging perspectives through a variety of encounters and tasks. You will be supported by crits, presentations on and off - site research and digital or material engagement and tutorials. moduleContent: Art Practice Futures seeks to evolve diverse art processes leading to actions, experiences, and outcomes new to students. In this module you will be expanding your knowledge of a context or learning situation that exists within an interdisciplinary framing. This offers you the opportunity to advance your understanding of known and alternative perspectives through engagement with relevant contemporary co- ordinates related to your strand of enquiry. This will embody a matrix of material, cultural, social or economic considerations, for example, with the momentum of the creative exchange centred upon present and future potentials, with awareness of resources, opportunities and challenges. Through your growing sophistication to act upon and nuance your discoveries, you will evolve your process toward an experimental art outcome to be shared with others. It is expected that you will be able to reflect particularly on the environment in which you are immersed. This should include an understanding of where the context sits within popular media. It is expected that you will learn to advance perspectives and that you will present this in areas related to the environment in which you have chosen to study. moduleContent: You will be introduced to working methods and professional practices within and beyond your specific discipline. The value inherent in these engagements is to learn of and share perspectives in relation to creative visual practices. It will normally require collaborative or team working and may require ‘real world’ engagement or/and interdisciplinary engagement. This module aims to: • Encourage consideration of different working contexts and situations. • Foster professional and creative responsibility and engagement. • Understand connections and relationships between fields of practice. • Develop an awareness of the relationship between art and audience.

moduleContent: Professional Practice offers learning challenges for students that expand their capacities and introduce further models of engagement and action to complement their understanding and experience. The module requires joint learning contexts for groups of students and staff creating an outward facing potential. The module requires student participation, engagement and the ability to work and coordinate with others in developing a creative momentum and output. The module aims to introduce students to working collaboratively in a situated context – the intensive is based around a set theme or location. For example, indicative themes are: site specificity; community and connectivity; sites of practice; infrastructure; distribution; systems and circuits. Depending on the headline knowledge domain, the intensive aims to immerse students in a 2-week learning experience that asks, for example, what does it mean to be site specific, across critical, material and discursive modes of engagement and practice. The intensive is taught primarily through workshops to encourage situated and hands-on learning with skills acquisition. The aims of this module are to: • Encourage active consideration of different working contexts and situations. • Deepen knowledge of working methods and professional practices within and beyond your specific discipline. • Develop opportunities for collaborative ethos and interdisciplinary engagement. • Foster broader and deeper professional and creative responsibility and engagement. • Understand diverse connections and relationships between fields of practice. • Develop a greater awareness of the relationship between art and audience. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year FA2-4 Professional Practice 4 Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 11 Sep 24 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD.
loText: PARTICIPATE: Demonstrate the ability to professionally and responsibly participate with a range of opportunities and contexts within and beyond the College.

moduleContent: In this module students develop competencies for independent thinking and learning, for self- directed research and for the establishment of professional art practices. Students develop a self- directed project for presentation and dissemination, and begin to locate their practice within current art discourse. They consider modes of making work, its production, and alternatives in relation to different ways of showing and sharing their work – its distribution. The aims of this module are to: • Encourage students to identify and develop research and interests in the making of work. • Demonstrate the ability to synthesise research into practice. • Enable students to develop and publicly present self- directed and collaborative work. • Encourage resolved and considered production and presentation. • Encourage and support collaborative enquiry and working. • Encourage students to consider different working contexts and audiences. • Work safely in relation to self and others.
loText: RESEARCH: Demonstrate the capacity to integrate research and personal interests in the development and production of artwork.

They consider modes of making work, its production, and alternatives in relation to different ways of showing and sharing their work – its distribution. The aims of this module are to: • Encourage students to identify and develop research and interests in the making of work. • Demonstrate the ability to synthesise research into practice. • Enable students to develop and publicly present self- directed and collaborative work. • Encourage resolved and considered production and presentation. • Encourage and support collaborative enquiry and working. • Encourage students to consider different working contexts and audiences. • Work safely in relation to self and others.
loText: CONSOLIDATE: Apply appropriate skills to art practice that demonstrate an understanding of methodologies and processes relevant to their concerns.

loText: DISSEMINATE: Evidence a critical understanding of the relationship between art, audience and context by situating work.

moduleContent: This Professional Practice module occurs throughout the final year of our undergraduate programme. It is concerned with guiding the learner through the broad and pluralist contexts of being an artist in Ireland and Europe today. Final year is the place for students to start thinking about their career and recognising that a professional artists’ career can be very varied and there is not a simple single occupation category. The aims of this module are to: • Develop advanced knowledge of the ‘ins and outs ‘of the professional world of art production, marketing, networking and reception. • Foster a level of critical and contextual judgement in students’ practice and in the practices of other artists, institutions, funders and networks. • Support and guide students to clarify their career plan in the art world taking account of its opportunities /supports/challenges. • Help students evolve their own peer led support networks for their future work and their development of good working practices. • Develop skills in documentation and recording of their work. • Learn how to become a reflective practitioner. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year (two trimesters) FA3-1 Professional Practice 5 Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council on: 11 Sep 24 ] Page 1 of 6 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD.

moduleContent: Studio Research is an integrated activity that underpins and informs the direction of studio practice. Through this module, you should understand research and practice to be inseparably linked. The module expects that through your learning about current art research, developing skills and techniques, undertaking and making research enquiries and engaging in research discussions you will develop an ongoing momentum in relation to developing your work. The aims of this module are to: ● Support the development and construction of knowledge new to the student. ● Foster methods of inquiry through art and other methods and that are relevant to the subject matter. ● Support the application of research through practice. ● Develop challenging relationships between the subject matter, forms of research, and forms of practice. The evidence of research outputs could be very varied - consistent with different ways of experimenting and communicating, for example: artworks artefacts material tests evolved proposals notebooks journals 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year (two trimesters) FA3-2 Fine Art Studio Research Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 11 Sep 24 Page 1 of 6 This document was prepared for NCAD students. moduleContent: In this year-long module you will evolve your work towards identifying the apex of your art practice and present it. For artists, the ability to communicate ideas through art practice is a key skill and capacity. Presentation of your work should be understood as an elastic term potentially embracing, enabling and deciding from a very wide spectrum of dissemination means. This means that in evolving your work there are a wide variety of possibilities that offer platforms for practice that reach audiences in different ways including familiar and alternative means. In this module you will evolve your art practice over two trimesters with an assessment point at the end of each Trimester with a related grade. This builds toward a final mark for this module. You will learn through making and evolving artwork informed further developing skills that are appropriate to communicating your emerging interests and themes and critical consideration of its presentation. This module sits parallel to your Studio Research module which frames the process of your art research, your testing of ideas through making, referencing, and critical reflection. The aims of this module are to: ● Bring self-directed art research and practice to presented /exhibited/ disseminated resolution. ● Foster critical engagement with and knowledge of a spectrum of contemporary exhibition forms. ● Encourage synthesis of conceptual, technical and material forms. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year (two trimesters) FA3-3 Fine Art Practice Resolution Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 11 Sep 24 Page 1 of 5 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD. ● Develop ambition and creative risk taking in making and testing exhibition components.
loText: RESOLVE: Present a coherent body of work to a professional standard that demonstrates relevant decision-making.

In this module you will evolve your art practice over two trimesters with an assessment point at the end of each Trimester with a related grade. This builds toward a final mark for this module. You will learn through making and evolving artwork informed further developing skills that are appropriate to communicating your emerging interests and themes and critical consideration of its presentation. This module sits parallel to your Studio Research module which frames the process of your art research, your testing of ideas through making, referencing, and critical reflection. The aims of this module are to: ● Bring self-directed art research and practice to presented /exhibited/ disseminated resolution. ● Foster critical engagement with and knowledge of a spectrum of contemporary exhibition forms. ● Encourage synthesis of conceptual, technical and material forms. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year (two trimesters) FA3-3 Fine Art Practice Resolution Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 11 Sep 24 Page 1 of 5 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD. ● Develop ambition and creative risk taking in making and testing exhibition components.

moduleContent: Professionalising the Practice Professional Practice modules are concerned with learning about the mechanics of negotiating, communicating and presentation of oneself to professional bodies and contexts outside of the College and preparing for future career and study strategies. The module offers ways to compile a clear cohesive portfolio, through written and digital formats. The components require active engagement with ‘live’ applications or bids and evidence of transferable skills relevant to the context of the application or bid. This module requires archived evidence and participation in NCAD lectures and with studio discipline seminars. This module is not to be seen merely to run parallel to the studio activities, nor solely to careers after college has finished. Instead, the Professional Practice module together with the Studio Research and Practice Resolution modules are holistically informing the nature of the practice. Along with ‘live‘ bids / applications, students will compile and build their CV, write Artist Statements and bios for their ‘portfolio pack’. Active attendance, in seminars and NCAD lecture series to be documented with investigation of themes and artists of interest, is expected throughout. The aims of delivering and studying Applied Materials Professional Practice are: • To develop advanced knowledge of the professional world of contemporary art and relevant design contexts. • To understand the transferrable skills gained that can be utilised in interdisciplinary contexts and careers. • To communicate to others the level of critical and contextual awareness and understanding at play in their work and in the work of others. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year FA3002: Applied Materials Professional Practice Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 10 Feb 21 Page 1 of 5 This document was prepared for NCAD students. moduleContent: In this module you will identify the apex of your art practice and present it in exhibition form. You are expected to demonstrate your ability to make and exhibit your work. Your exhibition is the form and subject of the examination. For Applied Material practitioners, the ability to communicate ideas is a key skill and capacity. The ‘exhibition’, should be understood as a term to be widely interpreted and inclusive of a very wide spectrum of art and interdisciplinary activities. This relates also to how and where contemporary artists choose to present their work. Hence gallery and site-specific contexts can be considered. In meeting the learning outcomes of the Practice Resolution module you are expected to bring forward pertinent art enquiries, experiments, tests, to a significant level of resolution that has been underpinned and informed by the Research module. Your proposed outcome should be managed and manageable but also be ambitious in how it communicates its concerns. You should consider who the artwork is for and how the viewer, user or audience is to engage in and experience the art outcomes. The Practice Resolution Module will develop an understanding of how artists construct, display, and set up art exhibits. Thus, the importance of understanding ‘context’ and how to present artworks to others is vital. There are different manifestations e.g. exhibition attendance, artist presentation, testing your own approaches and developing conceptual and technical know-how are all part of fully realising and resolving the artworks. Aims The aim of this module is to: • Bring self-directed art research and practice to exhibited resolution. • Foster critical engagement with and knowledge of a spectrum of contemporary exhibition forms. • Encourage synthesis of conceptual, technical and material forms. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year FA3003: Applied Art Practice Resolution Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 10 Feb 21 Page 1 of 5 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD. • Develop ambition and creative risk taking in making and testing exhibition components.

moduleContent: This module introduces learners to the nature of creative learning as articulated and theorized in teaching, learning and pedagogy scholarship. Through the analyses of cases studies, site visits, and pedagogical workshops, learners will have opportunities to deepen their knowledge of the qualities and potentialities of creative learning, which ought to contribute to their capacity to establish creative environments for learning in schools, community learning sites, museums, and other educational spaces. Acknowledging that teaching is a creative act that creates, this module seeks to equip educators with skills and knowledge that will enable them to build curriculum and enact teaching and learning practices that promote learning in, with and through creative practices and creative modes of studying and representing the world. Creative learning prioritizes exploration, experimentation and innovation. It seeks to cultivate creative, imaginative and critical thinking skills and practices. Creative learning is inquiry led, encouraging learners to take risks and value the educational potential of false starts and failures, of things not realized as imagined or anticipated. Thus, creative learning promotes collaborative, inquisitive and investigative learning and teaching and values wonderment and curiosity. It stimulates students’ curiosity to learn and explore through making, thinking and critical reflection. Recognising the affordance of creativity as a way of encountering, making sense of, and acting in the world, arts and educational policy and practice have embraced creative learning as a critical component of education in formal and informal learning spaces. Creative learning and creative pedagogies are increasingly prioritized in educational discourses across a range or learning and teaching sites. The module ought to appeal to a wide range of learners: educators at all levels from primary to further and higher education, managers and support workers in learning organisations; educational 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year PGCFAF1019 Creative Learning Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 24 Jun 25 Page 1 of 6 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD. consultants, specialists and managers in learning and development roles, current and aspiring lecturers, as well as creative and community practitioners. The aims of this module are to: ● Encourage an expanded view of creative learning and teaching, not least that undertaken by students on this module. ● Support a critical understanding of the emergence, reach, and significance of the concept and practice of creative learning and teaching across a range of educational sites. ● Equip educators with skills and knowledge that will enable them to build curriculum and enact teaching and learning practices that promote learning in, with and through creative practices and creative modes of studying and representing the world

moduleContent: This Creative Principles Arts and Health introduces you, the learner, to the fundamental principles, working methods, skills and theories that underpin contemporary arts and health practice in Ireland today. It will focus on contemporary issues, topics and concepts relating to Arts and Health practice enabling an approach to creative practice in health from a theoretically-informed perspective. Over the past thirty years, the field of arts and health has expanded across research, practice and policy, and a growing body of evidence indicates that creative activities are “powerful tools'' that enhance health, defined as “a state of complete physical, mental and social wellbeing and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity, thus rooting health firmly within society and culture” Learners are asked to investigate and critically reflect on the broad and creative approaches to art practice in health contexts identifying boundaries and the distinction between arts and health and art therapy, and will focus on the theoretical underpinnings, contemporary issues, topics and concepts relating to arts and health practice. The aims of this module are to: ● assist participants in developing their own creativity in relation to contemporary creative practice and discourse within Art and Health. ● provide participants with appropriate skills and knowledge that can be applied to their professional development as a creative practitioner within a health environment. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year PGCFAF1010 Creative Principles – Art & Health Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 22 Jun 23 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD.

moduleContent: Our contemporary world of digital and networked cultures raises issues and questions that can be purposefully addressed through Fine Art Media practices. Participants are challenged to engage critically with how meaning is made, how representations and encounters are produced, circulated, regulated and consumed in contemporary life, and act upon the ways in which value is constructed and understood by individuals and groups – in the contexts of local, national and global discourses. This Professional Certificate seeks to engage components selected from a larger hinterland of possible media practices and concerns. The module encourages participants to integrate research, creative practice and contemporary methods, theory and contexts. The forces and themes that underpin our understanding of contexts are supported by an emergent situational awareness that offers a rich and potentially restless matrix. The aims of this module are to: • Support practitioners with requisite knowledge and skills to creatively contextualise their practice and imaginatively demonstrate its wider validity. • Develop the participant’s skills in research and the translation of findings into their creative practice. • Critically evaluate and engage with contemporary debates regarding practice-based research, practice-led research and cognate creative methodologies. • Assist participants in developing their own practice in relation to contemporary creative practice and discourse. This may be as an artist, maker, curator, educator, writer or other. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year PGCFAF1018 Creative Principles – Digital Art & Contemporary Contexts Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 24 Apr 2024 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD.
loText: DEFINE: Demonstrate an outline knowledge and understanding of current media strategies and practices, utilised within contemporary art.

moduleContent: Our contemporary world of digital and networked cultures raises issues and questions that can be purposefully addressed through Fine Art Media practices. Participants are challenged to engage critically with how meaning is made, how representations and encounters are produced, circulated, regulated and consumed in contemporary life, and act upon the ways in which value is constructed and understood by individuals and groups – in the contexts of local, national and global discourses. This Professional Certificate seeks to engage components selected from a larger hinterland of possible media practices and concerns. The module encourages participants to integrate research, creative practice and contemporary methods, theory and contexts. The forces and themes that underpin our understanding of contexts are supported by an emergent situational awareness that offers a rich and potentially restless matrix. The aims of this module are to: • Support practitioners with requisite knowledge and skills to creatively contextualise their practice and imaginatively demonstrate its wider validity. • Develop the participant’s skills in research and the translation of findings into their creative practice. • Critically evaluate and engage with contemporary debates regarding practice-based research, practice-led research and cognate creative methodologies. • Assist participants in developing their own practice in relation to contemporary creative practice and discourse. This may be as an artist, maker, curator, educator, writer or other. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year PGCFAF1018 Creative Principles – Digital Art & Contemporary Contexts Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 24 Apr 2024 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD.

moduleContent: Students will learn the regulatory requirements and design controls that govern every stage of medical-device development. Through lectures and hands-on workshops, the module builds on their understanding of the regulatory authorities’ expectations and relevant international standards. Alongside this, students begin drafting their trimester 3 Thesis proposals, applying module learning to a real industry-collaborative project The aims of this module are to: • Map the medical-device regulatory landscape identifying key authorities, regulations, and standards that guide device approval and compliance. • Understand stage gates and technical hurdles recognising the critical review points and technical demonstrations required throughout the development pathway. • Use regulatory compliance tools to apply checklists, risk-management templates, and documentation strategies aligned with industry guidance. • Anticipate project specific regulatory needs to evaluate which regulations and standards will impact their upcoming summer-thesis projects. • Craft a regulation informed thesis proposal to develop a clear project plan and objectives that integrate regulatory considerations from the outset.

moduleContent: This module addresses the meaning of curriculum in education in terms of the principles, values and ideologies that underpin curriculum formation in various contexts and at different times. The ideologies, values and the various principles and priorities that may from time to time affect the shape and interpretation of the curriculum are discussed. The distinctions between the curriculum as designed, as interpreted and as received are explored, with particular reference to national and school-based curriculum development. Various models and approaches to curriculum design and development are examined. The particular relationship between curriculum and assessment is given special attention. Principles and functions of assessment within curriculum and policy are analysed. The aims of this module are to: ● Describe the main aspects of curriculum formation and the various types of curricula (formal, informal, hidden etc.). ● Explore curriculum change and consider factors that influence curricular provision at post- primary level. ● To understand the role of Art teacher in creating Visual Art curricula. ● Develop a deeper understanding of the Junior Cycle Framework in relation to Visual Art. ● Explore curriculum change at Senior Cycle. ● Consider factors that influence curricular provision at post-primary level. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year (two trimesters) PGED1003 Curriculum Studies Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 10 Sep 25 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD.

moduleContent: The aim of this module is to provide an introduction to the images, objects and concepts that constitute key current issues in visual and material culture. The module comprises of two types of class – a series of weekly lectures and a regular weekly seminar. The lectures do not approach art or design as separate categories: instead, they explore themes and issues which impact on all areas of practice at NCAD such as gender, the ethics of the representation of others, concepts of nature / the natural, etc. The primary purpose of the seminar is to provide students with opportunities to develop techniques of visual and textual analysis in the supportive context of a seminar. It will usually be based on a single text which the student is required to read in advance, coming to class prepared to discuss it. Some of these texts will be landmark works of critical analysis such as the work of Walter Benjamin; others will be more recent interventions such as the writings of Hito Steyerl. The aims of this module are to: 1. Provide an introduction to techniques and approaches of object, image and textual analysis.
loText: RESEARCH: Develop the skills required to identify conceptual sources suitable for the analysis of cultural forms.

moduleContent: Like Module VC1-1, the broad aim of this module is to provide an introduction to the images, objects and concepts that constitute major current issues in visual and material culture. And, like Module VC1-1, this comprises of two types of class – a series of weekly lectures and a regular weekly seminar. After the emphasis on identity which characterises the content on module VC1-1, this series of lectures and seminars focuses on a different but parallel set of themes including sustainability, the ethics of production, and the Anthropocene. The seminar provides opportunities for the students to develop and test techniques of visual and textual analysis in a supportive context. It will usually be based on a single text which the student is required to read in advance, coming to class prepared for discussion of it. Some of these texts will be historical works by major philosophers of modernity. Others will offer a contemporary perspective. Emphasis will be placed on the effects and legacies of modernism/modernity in the present. Students will submit an essay at the end of the trimester which will present an opportunity to critically reflect on the works and ideas which have featured in this module. The aims of this module are to: ● Introduce students to key current themes and debates in art and design today. ● Introduce students to techniques of visual and object analysis. ● Provide opportunities to test concepts and theories. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year VC2-1 Introduction to Key Concepts in Art & Design 1B Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 15 Nov 23 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. moduleContent: Visual Culture developed from the long established fields of Art and Design History and Cultural Studies as well as more specialist disciplines like Film Studies. This module introduces students to some of the key concepts and methods in Art and Design History. It is therefore a second introductory module and follows VC1-4 Introduction to Visual Culture. Students will also be introduced to paradigmatic examples of objects and practices relevant to the study of Art and Design. Students will also consider works of art and design in their wider contexts using appropriate methods of analysis to establish both theoretical and contextual frameworks such, for instance, as style. The question of how a canon is formed and its effects will be explored, as will established art and design historical concepts such as originality, techniques, and iconography. The aims of this module are to develop critical understanding of: ● Definitions of art and design, and how these are situated within particular intellectual traditions, histories, and canons. ● Key historical categories and concepts in Art and Design history including: Classicism; Renaissance; Baroque; Modernism. ● Key methods and tools of analysis in Art and Design History such as ‘object analysis’.
loText: RESEARCH: Identify and apply an appropriate range of key historical definitions and concepts used in the History of Art and Design.

moduleContent: The purpose of this module is to introduce and contextualize various technologies and environments relating to the study of visual culture. Surveying a broad range of representational and distributive technologies including print, photography, cinema and the internet, classes in this module set out to explore the extent to which different formats influence and even determine cultural practices. As a concept, technology is synonymous with new or high technology. Through historical case studies, this module sets out to explore the ways in which traces and forms of older technologies persist in newer ones. Drawing on scholarship in the field of media archaeologies, it also pays particularly attention to the material forms of these technologies. This module will establish an historical and theoretical background for the constantly changing technologies, concepts and media forms of digital systems – the subject of a module in year two (VC2-6). The aims of this module are to: 1. Introduce and contextualize technologies and environments relating to the study of visual culture.
loText: RESEARCH: Demonstrate an applied knowledge of the role played by different technologies in particular aspects of the production, distribution and/or consumption of forms of visual culture.

moduleContent: This module introduces students to materials, processes and techniques of making employed by designers and craftspeople today and in the past. It will approach processes and techniques of making as repositories of knowledge about the potential and tolerances of materials. As such, it will allow students to understand how materiality sets limits on matters like the scale or durability of things. The module will span a range of techniques and processes from long-established practices like weaving and intaglio printing to digital techniques such as 3-D printing. It will examine the extent to which such new technologies and processes contain within them histories of older ways of making and knowing. A key aim of this module is to ensure that students have a heightened understanding of the nature of materials, processes and techniques that will benefit other aspects of their studies in Visual Culture. Other aims of this module are to: 1. Introduce and contextualize a wide range of materials, processes and techniques of making that have been employed by designers and craftspeople in historical and contemporary contexts;

moduleContent: This module introduces students to the field of Visual Culture. In particular students are introduced to key debates and central figures who have shaped the field since the 1990s. Students will test different approaches to diverse image practices drawn from modern and contemporary Visual Culture encompassing vernacular, commercial and visual art contexts. Students will, in this way, develop key image-analysis skills. They will also be taught how to apply concepts in the practice of image-analysis. Such concepts include remediation, hauntology, performativity, post-colonial theory, and spectacle. This module is offered before a second introductory module on art and design historical methods and concepts such as style, expression, originality. In the assessment task, a presentation, particular emphasis will be placed on developing an understanding of the approaches of key figures in Visual Culture studies including Laura Mulvey, Hito Steyerl, Mark Fisher, Lev Manovich, Stuart Hall, Jack Halberstam, Susan Sontag, Giuliana Bruno and WTJ Mitchell. As a presentation, students will be engaged in sharing perspectives and understanding with their peers. The module is supported by an emphasis on learning presentation skills. The aims of this module are to develop critical understanding of: 1. Different approaches to Visual Culture, and how these are situated within particular intellectual traditions and histories.
loText: RESEARCH: Identify and apply an appropriate range of key historical definitions and concepts used in the field of Visual Culture studies.

moduleContent: The purpose of this module is to introduce professional practice skills relating to the study of Visual Culture. The module complements the critical study of art and design undertaken in other modules of the Visual Culture degree by considering the various ways that the interpretive and expressive tools analysed elsewhere on the programme have different practical applications in specific professional situations. It is also the first of a number of professional practice modules offered as part of the BA Visual Culture degree and, as such, explores the ways in which art and design are mediated by a wide range of different bodies, institutions and mediums, and the professional techniques and interests at work in each of these contexts. Later professional practice modules will offer greater opportunities for deeper study. In Professional Practice: Mediation consideration will be given to the ways in which different mediating organisations – such as public and private museums and galleries, film festivals, auction houses, and art, design and film magazines and periodicals – confer value on works of art and design. It will also give consideration to the role of arts organisations in funding and promoting these sectors. Students will also be encouraged to become attentive to the different languages and forms of expression employed by these mediators, to reflect on the differences between advocacy and critique. This will be tested by the range of texts produced by each student for assessment. Students will be asked to examine different writing modes – including promotional texts such as exhibition press releases, as well as critical texts such as reviews – through practical exercises. The broad aims of this module are to: ● To understand the position and function of different arts organisations in relation to visual culture, art, design and film practice; ● To understand how value is conferred on works of visual culture, art, design and film by different institutions and organisations; 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year VC1002 Professional Practice: Mediation Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council on 15 Nov 23 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. loText: RESEARCH: Develop skills needed to generate and evaluate content, strategies and audience relationships appropriate to a range of relevant professional contexts.

moduleContent: Visual Culture developed from the long established fields of Art and Design History and Cultural Studies as well as more specialist disciplines like Film Studies. This module introduces students to some of the key concepts and methods in Art and Design History. It is therefore a second introductory module and follows VC1001 Introduction to Visual Culture. Students will also be introduced to paradigmatic examples of objects and practices relevant to the study of Art and Design. Students will also consider works of art and design in their wider contexts using appropriate methods of analysis to establish both theoretical and contextual frameworks such, for instance, as style. The question of how a canon is formed and its effects will be explored, as will established art and design historical concepts such as originality, techniques, and iconography. The aims of this module are to develop critical understanding of: ● Definitions of art and design, and how these are situated within particular intellectual traditions, histories, and canons. ● Key historical categories and concepts in Art and Design history including: Classicism; Renaissance; Baroque; Modernism. ● Key methods and tools of analysis in Art and Design History such as ‘object analysis’.

moduleContent: The purpose of this module is to support students to understand and critically examine highly situated approaches to Visual Culture. Focusing on Dublin, where most of the students are located and all study, the module explores the ways in which objects of visual culture are shaped by situation – understood broadly in terms of history, urban infrastructure, demography, cultural institutions and so on. As such the module acts as an important introduction to a key aspect of Visual Culture studies but also to the city. Students will be encouraged to reflect on their own ‘local’ situation and, where appropriate, to engage with primary evidence of various kinds including interviews, archival collections, documentary photographs, etc. Emphasis will also be placed in the changing face of the city, and students will have opportunities to research and analyse different locations. They will be encouraged to develop their own critical perspectives on this material by using a range of conceptual and theoretical tools discussed in class and in the readings provided. This module aims to: 1. Encourage students to understand and critically reflect on the roles played by both professional and amateur practices in the shaping of culture;
loText: RESEARCH: explore amateur and under-recorded social phenomena in specific settings;

moduleContent: The aim of this module is to support students in developing an understanding of a key field of Visual Culture practice – the exhibition – prior to key modules such as the ‘placement’ in trimester 2 of final year. Students will propose a project – in consultation with module staff – that culminates in a portfolio of writing and a visual display for a thematic exhibition. The display may be realised or it might take the form of a worked-up and detailed proposal. Through seminar workshops and lecture sessions they will work towards developing an appropriate plan for the realization of this real or fictional project. Where appropriate, students will be given some practical training in software such as Sketch-up. Students will need to take account of questions relating to processes of production, display and distribution, considering the theoretical and ideological issues raised by their project plan, in addition to considering how their project might meet accepted professional standards and relating to existing models in the field. Live case studies will be presented by guests tutors on the programme with experience of Museum professionals in the fields of curating; exhibition planning and management; engagement and learning; dissemination and interpretation; and marketing.” The aims of this module are: 1. To provide an opportunity for students to test their ideas about the potential of exhibition as the form of intellectual enquiry;

moduleContent: The aim of this module is to engage students with current theories relevant to contemporary practices in art and design. Students on this module select one thematic weekly seminar course from a range of 12-14 options. This is known as an ‘elective’. Not organized according to a single medium or field of art / design practice but by concepts and themes, the seminar also allows students from different disciplines to exchange ideas and perspectives. It provides an introduction to the intellectual and analytical depth required to undertake the research projects which NCAD BA students are required to undertake in their second and third year of study. The seminars present students with the opportunity to: 1. Acquire a critical understanding of current theories and concepts relating to local and international art and design practices.

moduleContent: The aim of this module is to engage students with current theories relevant to contemporary practices in art and design. Students on this module select one thematic weekly seminar course from a range of 12-14 options. This is known as an ‘elective’. Not organized according to a single medium or field of art / design practice but by concepts and themes, the seminar also allows students from different disciplines to exchange ideas and perspectives. It provides an introduction to the intellectual and analytical depth required to undertake the research projects which NCAD BA students are required to undertake in their second and third year of study. The seminars present students with the opportunity to: 1. Acquire a critical understanding of current theories and concepts relating to local and international art and design practices.
loText: ANALYSE: Demonstrate a critical understanding of several areas of contemporary practice in art and design that enable evaluation and critical reflection on own work and field whether as an artist or designer, or as a writer and critical thinker.

moduleContent: This module develops and extends students’ knowledge of the phenomenon of modernism in different fields of art and design through the close examination of different cultural phenomena. It will also provide a framework for the analysis of different interpretative frameworks for understanding modernism and modernity. In approaching modernism through a variety of frameworks and perspectives - including formal analysis; influence; cultural and historical significance; relationship to social, historical and cultural contexts; contemporary relevance - the module sets out to explore the complexity and even contradictory claims made for the phenomenon of modernism. It follows on from a first-year module in which the broad historical category of modernity is put under scrutiny (VC1-5). In this way students will be developing greater understanding of historiography. The class takes the form of a lecture followed by a seminar in which various kinds of historical evidence will be examined (film, textual documents, etc.) each week. The aims of this module are to: 1. Introduce students to key examples that exemplify and define Modernism;
loText: RESEARCH key concepts, theories and methodologies that have informed art and design in the 20th and 21st century, including paradigmatic examples relevant to thinking and writing about modern and contemporary art and design practices. moduleContent: The purpose of this module is to support the ongoing development of professional practice skills pertaining to the study of visual culture. To achieve this broad aim, the module concentrates on professional practice contexts in Ireland, considering ways in which subjects studied more broadly within this programme can be applied to the work of appropriate local institutions and agencies. The module makes use of an extended case study (or, if appropriate, a number of case studies) in order to support students in developing their understanding of institutional conditions that shape visual culture in Ireland. In particular, it focuses on the role played by the institution/s in the organisation and dissemination of knowledge of visual culture beyond matters of exhibition and display (these concerns feature strongly in other professional practice modules offered in years 1 and 3). As such, it places particular emphasis on organisations like archives and libraries, as well as the tools central to their activities such as databases and catalogues. Central to these studies is the question of how specific institutions and agencies in Ireland seek to understand and administer key aspects of their operations such as audiences and publics, collection policy, outreach and education. How the practices of local and national organisations are informed by international relationships and tendencies is also an important point of reference. Each year an organisation / number of related organisations will be approached to offer students access to their collections, staff and organisation systems. Where possible, classes will take place in situ as well as on the NCAD site. Research tasks will be designed in partnership with the staff of these institutions to develop insights and understanding of their operations and, if successful, new knowledge about objects and documents in their care. The aims of this module are to: 1. moduleContent: The purpose aim of the module is to familiarise students with the key issues in digital media and digital culture and related theoretical/philosophical concerns. It constitutes a more focused examination of the role of digital technology in Visual Culture after the VC1-6 module offered in the first year (Technologies of Visual Culture). This module examines the relationship between the virtual and the real, issues of human identity and relationship, the nature of life and intelligence in the context of cybernetics, political issues around surveillance and privacy in the digital era, the relationship between cybernetics and aesthetics, in particular with regard to notions of the sublime (Burke, Kant) in the context of cyberspace, and the Gesamtkunstwerk or total art work (Wagner) in the context of virtual reality. The module addresses issues around computer games in the context of telepresence and augmented reality, and focuses on specific cultural examples of digital art/design, including computer animation, interactive art, crossover art/design/technology, and digital music and examines emerging cultural phenomena. The aims of this module are: 1. To provide a set of critical frameworks for understanding the role of digital media and digital culture in different fields of culture.
loText: RESEARCH: Develop an overview of several key philosophical issues pertaining to digital culture, including the relationship between the virtual and the real, the nature of human identity and relationships, the implications for issues in aesthetics. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year VC2-6 Understanding Digital Culture Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council 15 Nov 23 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD.

moduleContent: The purpose of this module is to provide a context where many of the writing, research and analytical skills which have been developed by the third year of study on the programme can be exploited and tested. In particular, it allows for students to focus interests that they have developed in the broad field of Visual Culture. Typically, this module will focus on Dublin since the Celtic Tiger (Other ‘situations’ will be introduced to allow for comparison). Rather than viewing the production of art and design narrowly as the works of individual agents, the module examines the extent to which creativity of various kinds might be understood as a collective and historical phenomenon across different forms / genres of cultural expression. Key settings include the art school and the nightclub, as well as the gallery and the academy. It also explores the effects of class, power and hegemonic forms of culture on the emergence of non-conformist of sub-cultural phenomena. Extending beyond the professional settings of the gallery or the museum, it sets out to understand and evaluate the impact and forms of ‘amateur’ cultural products such as the fanzine or independent music labels. Students will be encouraged to engage with primary evidence of various kinds including interviews, archival collections, documentary photographs, etc. Emphasis will also be placed in the changing face of the city, and students will have opportunities to research and analyse different locations. They will be encouraged to develop their own critical perspectives on this material by using a range of conceptual and theoretical tools from Visual Culture studies. This module aims to: 1. Encourage students to understand and critically reflect on the roles played by both professional and amateur practices in the shaping of culture;
loText: RESEARCH: explore amateur and under-recorded social phenomena;

It also explores the effects of class, power and hegemonic forms of culture on the emergence of non-conformist of sub-cultural phenomena. Extending beyond the professional settings of the gallery or the museum, it sets out to understand and evaluate the impact and forms of ‘amateur’ cultural products such as the fanzine or independent music labels. Students will be encouraged to engage with primary evidence of various kinds including interviews, archival collections, documentary photographs, etc. Emphasis will also be placed in the changing face of the city, and students will have opportunities to research and analyse different locations. They will be encouraged to develop their own critical perspectives on this material by using a range of conceptual and theoretical tools from Visual Culture studies. This module aims to: 1. Encourage students to understand and critically reflect on the roles played by both professional and amateur practices in the shaping of culture;
loText: COMMUNICATE & ORGANISE: be able to develop an appropriate form of presentation for their findings.

moduleContent: The study of Visual Culture is located at the interface of objects, images, materials, technology, society, politics and economies. While other modules prioritise, for example, the study of technological or social histories, this module focuses on the economic dimensions of Visual Culture. It aims to explore the ways in which economic factors impact, and are impacted on, by Visual Culture. Although not presented as a formal training in the principles and techniques of the discipline of economics, this module will provide insights into the way in which government and economic policy – including the effects of neoliberalism and globalisation and forms of resistance to it, such as commoning practices – shape the production, dissemination and consumption of art and design. Economic and cultural imperatives can be seen as two of the most significant forces shaping human behaviour. The Economies of Visual Culture module presents an opportunity to consider the dynamic relationship between them as they act upon intellectual discourses, systems of societal organisation, and individual behaviour. Historic and contemporary case studies offered for analysis in the seminars will allow for claims about the new centrality and prominence of the culture industries and the knowledge economy in Ireland and elsewhere today to be tested. The aims of this module are to: 1. Survey, using paradigmatic examples, the history of the changing relationships between practices of the production of visual culture and their markets;
loText: RESEARCH the impact of economics on particular fields of visual culture and, conversely, the ways in which economic practices are themselves influenced by visual culture.

moduleContent: The purpose of the Placement module is to provide students with real world experience prior to completing their BA studies. Placements with selected partners in the public and private cultural sector relate to the Visual Culture curriculum and provide students with the opportunity to explore one of the numerous professional sectors which reside around the the fields of the arts, design, heritage and film, including arts administration, project management, curation, arts education, critical and popular writing, archiving, marketing and advertising. Assessment of this module is based on the student’s presentation and submission of annotated presentation slides, which critically reflects on the experience of the placement and is assessed by the placement supervisor. This will be an academic member of the School of Visual Culture. Work placement agreements are put in place between NCAD and each placement host and between NCAD and the student and should be agreed prior to the start of the placement. The work placement agreement between NCAD and the student includes a learning contract that establishes broad learning objectives while on placement. The length of the placement may vary within weeks 1-12 of trimester 2 and may comprise 1-day a week for 12 weeks or 2-days a week for 6 weeks. Provision for an alternative placement opportunity within NCAD (with an organisation such as NIVAL EML or NCAD Gallery) will be established in the event that a student is unable to secure an external placement. Should it prove impossible to undertake a placement in any given year, students will have an opportunity to undertake an equivalent module with comparable learning outcomes. The aims of this module are to: • Provide opportunities for students on the programme to understand and contribute the operations of an organisation related to Visual Culture. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year VC3-3 Visual Culture Placement Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 15 Nov 23 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. moduleContent: The aim of this module is to engage students with current theories relevant to contemporary practices in art and design. Students on this module select one thematic weekly seminar course from a range of 12-14 options. This is known as an ‘elective’. Not organized according to a single medium or field of art / design practice but by concepts and themes, the seminar also allows students from different disciplines to exchange ideas and perspectives. It provides an introduction to the intellectual and analytical depth required to undertake the research projects which NCAD BA students are required to undertake in their second and third year of study. The seminars present students with the opportunity to: • Acquire a critical understanding of current theories and concepts relating to local and international art and design practices. • Develop the analytical skills to understand and critique appropriate theories. • Develop further critical writing and research skills with reference to contemporary theories and practices. • Establish a theoretical and methodological framework for developing critical reflection on their own practice. 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year. moduleContent: The purpose of this project is to provide an opportunity for students to explore the potential and limits of forms of collaboration through a series of practical tasks as well as conceptual frameworks. As such it takes the form of workshops, seminars involving analysis of texts and concepts, and presentations by guest speakers, as well as the design of and engagement in a collaborative project. The module is organised so that it moves in weekly classes over the trimester from theory to practice in the expectation that students will be able to critically reflect on the ideas which they have been engaged with in the early classes in their collaborative practice and, conversely, that their practice will be informed by their conceptual thinking. Every student will be expected to present on the development of their project at regular intervals, and to fully document all aspects of the collaboration – this might take the form of keeping a record of emails, minutes of meetings and financial data. To successfully complete this module, students will be required to design a project which requires collaboration and to seek out collaborators. It might take the form of an event, a publication, a display or a service and ought to have a connection with the broad concerns and themes of Visual Culture. The module coordinator will provide support brokering and facilitating partnerships within NCAD and/or the city in the event that it is difficult for a student to establish his or her own. The module coordinator will also provide guidance about the scale and feasibility of any project being proposed. It is important to stress that whilst this is a live project which may well have public outcome/s, it need not be a success in conventional terms. Critical and informed reflection on the difficulties and challenges of collaboration may well demonstrate the independence and understanding which this module sets out to foster. As a third-year module, this is a culmination of the student’s critical and professional skills developed across the programme. It also allows the student to demonstrate independence in the design of the project and the nature of the collaboration undertaken. moduleContent: This final year Special Subject module presents students with an opportunity to explore current themes in the field of Visual Culture. Particular emphasis will be placed on emerging and changing matters of concern which are the focus of current critical thinking, and the module will, as such, place a strong emphasis on the contemporary even when addressing historical issues. Students on the module will engage with current and emerging theories of Visual Culture, as well as pressing debates about its effects on society, on the non-human, on the environment, etc. This module will be offered as a choice of two or more specialist classes which address different aspects of the field. For instance, it might be offered as a choice of a class on Global Cinema (addressing how matters of current concern such as human rights, climate change, diasporic culture, the migrant crisis, post colonialism, inter-generational conflict and economic injustice have been tackled by feature film and documentary film makers in different international settings), and another class on Future Ecology (exploring climate justice activism, sustainable exhibition-making practices, queer landscapes, biodesign, etc). The choice of topics will change regularly and will benefit from current expertise and research practices of staff in the School. As final year students, leaners will be expected to shape their learning in an active manner, writing and researching a question (assignment) of their own design and making a presentation to their peers. Particular emphasis will be placed on further developing the kinds of research skills required to understand contemporary and changing concerns, such as interviews with key actors, analysis of reports and policy documents, news reports, etc. Where possible, the special subject classes will connect with external organisations, individuals and partners working in the fields addressed. For instance, scheduled in the spring, the programme will coincide with the annual Dublin International Film Festival (DIFF) and close relations between the 1 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System, where 60 ECTS credits equate to the workload of a full- time academic year VC3004 Visual Culture Special Subject Module Descriptor Approved by Academic Council: 15 Nov 23 Page 1 of 4 This document was prepared for NCAD students. It may not be reproduced in whole or part without permission from NCAD. DIFF programmers and the School of Visual Culture will allow for screening opportunities and talks from film-makers and film industry professionals to be enjoyed by students. The aims of this module are to: • Engage in a deep and sustained fashion with contemporary concerns and debates in a field of Visual Culture. • Support students to extend their command of forms and methods of research of matters of contemporary concern. • Further develop the skills required to design and then answer a complex research question.
loText: UNDERSTAND and ANALYSE pressing issues in a field of contemporary visual culture, testing and applying key concepts and theories.

moduleContent: This final year Special Subject module presents students with an opportunity to explore current themes in the field of Visual Culture. Particular emphasis will be placed on emerging and changing matters of concern which are the focus of current critical thinking, and the module will, as such, place a strong emphasis on the contemporary even when addressing historical issues. Students on the module will engage with current and emerging theories of Visual Culture, as well as pressing debates about its effects on society, on the non-human, on the environment, etc. This module will be offered as a choice of two or more specialist classes which address different aspects of the field. For instance, it might be offered as a choice of a class on Global Cinema (addressing how matters of current concern such as human rights, climate change, diasporic culture, the migrant crisis, post colonialism, inter-generational conflict and economic injustice have been tackled by feature film and documentary film makers in different international settings), and another class on Future Ecology (exploring climate justice activism, sustainable exhibition-making practices, queer landscapes, biodesign, etc). The choice of topics will change regularly and will benefit from current expertise and research practices of staff in the School. As final year students, leaners will be expected to shape their learning in an active manner, writing and researching a question (assignment) of their own design and making a presentation to their peers. Particular emphasis will be placed on further developing the kinds of research skills required to understand contemporary and changing concerns, such as interviews with key actors, analysis of reports and policy documents, news reports, etc. Where possible, the special subject classes will connect with external organisations, individuals and partners working in the fields addressed. DIFF programmers and the School of Visual Culture will allow for screening opportunities and talks from film-makers and film industry professionals to be enjoyed by students. The aims of this module are to: • Engage in a deep and sustained fashion with contemporary concerns and debates in a field of Visual Culture. • Support students to extend their command of forms and methods of research of matters of contemporary concern. • Further develop the skills required to design and then answer a complex research question.
loText: DEVELOP and APPLY appropriate research tools for the study of an issue or matter of the student’s own choosing.

moduleContent: This module – offered in the final year of the BA Visual Culture degree – presents a significant opportunity for students to engage with topical and changing perspectives on nature and the concept of the natural in diverse fields of Visual Culture. Designed to support students who, at this stage of their studies, may have developed a particular interest in a field of visual culture such as in performance art, film, fashion or photography, it presents an occasion to undertake a focused piece of research and analysis of their own choosing, testing the concepts and ideas that have been developed in class. Connecting closely to current concerns about the Anthropocene, students will develop an understanding of changing attitudes to nature and the natural in different historical periods and diverse geographies. As such, this final phase module in their studies may provide an opportunity for critical reappraisal of images, spaces and objects which they have already encountered at an earlier stage.
loText: UNDERSTAND current critical debates about notions of the natural and the post-natural.
